entities.name: Acute kidney injury
entities.notes: Acute reduction in renal function requiring urgent stabilisation.
sourceMentions: AGEP 1 Potassium and Other Electrolyes and Minerals 24-25.pptx
Cardiology 5 - Approach to the small animal cardiac patient part A Handout.pptx
Types of fluids and routes of administration(1).pptx
pathogenesis: Sudden renal tubular, interstitial, vascular, or toxic injury causes abrupt loss of glomerular filtration and fluid-electrolyte homeostasis.
Complications include azotaemia, hyperkalaemia, acidosis, oliguria/anuria, and systemic uraemic effects.
managementPlan: Identify and remove the trigger, restore perfusion, manage fluid and electrolyte disturbances, monitor urine production, and use renal-toxic drugs with extreme caution or avoid them.

entities.notes: Chronic allergic inflammatory skin disease.

managementPlan: Use multimodal chronic management with trigger control, skin support, and treatment of secondary complications.

entities.notes: Chronic multisystem disease caused by Leishmania infantum.
sourceMentions: Lecture 7 - Leishmania intro, lifecycle, and disease.pptx
pathogenesis: Persistent intracellular protozoal infection drives chronic immune dysregulation, granulomatous inflammation, skin disease, and renal injury.
Disease severity depends heavily on host immune response.
managementPlan: Stage the disease, assess kidneys and proteinuria, then use long-term control rather than assuming true sterilising cure. Vector prevention matters.

entities.name: Chronic kidney disease
entities.notes: Progressive chronic loss of renal function.
sourceMentions: AGEP 1 Potassium and Other Electrolyes and Minerals 24-25.pptx
AGEP 2 BVSc 3 Chronic Kidney Disease and Proteinuria 25-26 HANDOUT.pptx

Clinical Pathology Approach to PUPD 1 page per slide PDF.pdf

pathogenesis: Progressive loss of functioning nephrons reduces renal reserve and impairs urine concentrating ability, excretion, and endocrine renal functions.
Ongoing nephron loss promotes azotaemia, uraemia, mineral imbalance, and often progression of proteinuria and hypertension.
managementPlan: Stage using IRIS-style principles, correct dehydration carefully, assess proteinuria and blood pressure, and tailor renal diet and supportive care to the patient.

entities.notes: Mite-associated follicular skin disease.

pathogenesis: Relative or absolute insulin deficiency impairs glucose uptake and promotes hyperglycaemia, glucosuria, osmotic diuresis, and altered fat metabolism.

managementPlan: Confirm persistent diabetes, stabilise emergencies such as ketoacidosis, then use insulin with diet, monitoring, and owner education.

entities.notes: Exercise-related bleeding from the lower airways in horses.
pathogenesis: High transmural capillary pressures during strenuous exercise contribute to pulmonary capillary stress failure and bleeding.
managementPlan: Assess athletic relevance, concurrent airway disease, and management context, then discuss exercise, race-day, and underlying airway strategies.

entities.notes: Severe chronic inflammatory oral syndrome in cats, often associated with caudal oral inflammation.
sourceMentions: 2025-26 Dentistry 3 SA extractions and Surgical Treatment Options.pptx
2025-26 Dentistry 7 Step-by-Step Dental LIVE (lecture version, with answers).pptx
pathogenesis: Severe chronic oral inflammation reflects complex immune dysregulation and antigenic stimulation, often with concurrent viral or plaque-associated contributors.
managementPlan: Control pain, assess retroviral/viral context, optimise plaque control, and discuss the role of partial or full-mouth extractions in refractory disease.

entities.notes: Severe coronavirus-associated disease driven by immune/inflammatory responses.
sourceMentions: Respiratory Medicine 3 The Dyspnoeic Patient Part B Handout.pptx
pathogenesis: Coronavirus mutation/tropism and host immune response produce pyogranulomatous vasculitis and either effusive or non-effusive multisystem disease.
The syndrome is driven heavily by immune-mediated and inflammatory consequences.
managementPlan: Diagnosis often relies on combining history, clinicopathology, imaging, and fluid/tissue interpretation. Discuss prognosis and current therapeutic context carefully.

pathogenesis: This is a syndrome rather than one disease and may reflect idiopathic cystitis, urolithiasis, urethral obstruction, infection, or neoplasia.
Stress, inflammation, crystals/stones, and urethral blockage can all contribute to the clinical picture.
managementPlan: Differentiate obstructed from non-obstructed cases first, provide analgesia and stabilisation, then investigate the underlying cause and recurrence risk.

entities.notes: Common feline infectious respiratory syndrome.
sourceMentions: POST SESSION Feline infectious diseases seminar S TASKER 2025-26 incl. Menti with answers.pptx

pathogenesis: Viral upper-airway infection causes conjunctivitis, rhinitis, ulceration, and variable systemic illness, with stress and crowding driving spread.
Secondary bacterial colonisation and chronic carrier states can complicate disease.
managementPlan: Provide nursing, hydration, and ocular/oral care, treat secondary bacterial complications when appropriate, and use vaccination as part of prevention rather than cure.

entities.name: Otitis externa
entities.notes: External ear canal inflammation often with secondary infection.
pathogenesis: Primary triggers such as allergy, parasites, foreign material, or conformation lead to inflammation, with secondary infection and chronic canal change following.
Chronicity alters the canal and makes recurrent disease harder to control.
managementPlan: Identify primary, secondary, predisposing, and perpetuating factors rather than treating the discharge alone. Topical therapy and cleaning should fit the diagnosed cause.

entities.name: Proteinuria
entities.notes: Marker and mediator of renal disease progression.
sourceMentions: AGEP 2 BVSc 3 Chronic Kidney Disease and Proteinuria 25-26 HANDOUT.pptx
Clinical Pathology Approach to PUPD - WITH ANSWERS.pptx
Clinical Pathology Approach to PUPD 1 page per slide PDF.pdf
pathogenesis: Abnormal glomerular permeability or reduced tubular reuptake allows excessive protein loss into urine.
Persistent proteinuria contributes to further renal damage and can accompany systemic hypertension or inflammatory renal disease.
managementPlan: Localise the source of protein loss, quantify severity, assess concurrent kidney disease and blood pressure, and treat the underlying cause while reducing ongoing glomerular injury.

entities.name: Rabies
entities.notes: Fatal zoonotic lyssavirus encephalitis.
sourceMentions: AGEP 2 BVSc 3 Travelling Dogs and Global Diseases 25-26.pptx
POST SESSION Feline infectious diseases seminar S TASKER 2025-26 incl. Menti with answers.pptx
Sustainable control of parasites in small animals.pptx
pathogenesis: After inoculation, lyssavirus travels via peripheral nerves to the CNS, causing progressive encephalitis and then centrifugal spread including salivary glands.
Disease is almost uniformly fatal once clinical signs develop.
managementPlan: This is a public-health emergency, not a routine therapeutic problem. Immediate isolation, legal reporting, and exposure management are the priorities.

sourceMentions: Sustainable control of parasites in farm animals - 2025.pptx
pathogenesis: Mite infestation triggers intense hypersensitivity and inflammatory skin damage, driving pruritus, fleece loss, and flock spread.
The disease is highly contagious and biosecurity is central.
managementPlan: Treat the whole epidemiologic group appropriately, address resistance risk, and integrate movement, diagnosis, and flock biosecurity control.

entities.mechanism: Centrally acting skeletal-muscle relaxation used as an anaesthetic adjunct
entities.mechanismGroup: Centrally acting skeletal-muscle relaxation used as an anaesthetic adjunct
commonUse: Most relevant as a muscle-relaxant adjunct in equine injectable or field anaesthesia, especially triple-drip style protocols.
Useful when you want smoother equine recumbency and muscle relaxation as part of a combined protocol.
avoidUse: Do not think of it as a sole anaesthetic plan because it is an adjunct rather than a full induction/maintenance agent.
Use carefully when airway control, recumbency management, or overall anaesthetic support are limited.
alternatives: Use inhalational maintenance or a different balanced injectable protocol when a triple-drip style plan is not appropriate.
Choose mainstream small-animal induction agents like propofol or alfaxalone when the patient is not an equine field-anaesthesia case.
entities.whyChoose: Compared with standard small-animal induction drugs, guaifenesin is chosen for its role in equine combined injectable protocols rather than as a standalone anaesthetic.

commonUse: Common inhalational maintenance agent across dogs, cats, horses, rabbits, and many exotics.
Useful when you want a familiar, widely available volatile with predictable maintenance of general anaesthesia.
avoidUse: Use carefully in hypotensive or very cardiovascularly unstable patients because volatile agents reduce blood pressure in a dose-dependent way.
Mask or chamber induction is generally avoided in common domestic species unless there is a specific reason.
alternatives: Choose sevoflurane when you want faster changes in depth or smoother inhalational induction/recovery characteristics.
Use more IV-sparing balanced anaesthesia or TIVA when volatile-related cardiovascular depression is a bigger concern.
entities.whyChoose: Compared with sevoflurane, isoflurane is often the practical default because it is familiar and commonly available, but it is less responsive for rapid depth changes.

commonUse: Useful as part of balanced anaesthesia for induction, restraint, or analgesic adjunct use rather than as the only drug in a polished modern protocol.
Commonly chosen when you want dissociative anaesthesia, preservation of some sympathetic tone, or wind-up prevention / analgesic support.
avoidUse: Use carefully where increased muscle tone, rough recovery, or seizure concerns would be a problem if ketamine is used without appropriate adjuncts.
Use carefully in selected cardiac, intracranial, or poorly stabilised patients depending on the overall case and protocol.
alternatives: Usually pair ketamine with a benzodiazepine such as diazepam or midazolam to improve muscle relaxation and induction quality.
Often combine it with an alpha-2 agonist, opioid, or both when you want stronger sedation, restraint, and multimodal analgesia.
Choose propofol or alfaxalone instead when you want smoother titratable IV induction in a routine dog or cat without relying on dissociative effects.
entities.whyChoose: Ketamine is often chosen when you want a dissociative agent that contributes analgesia and can be built into multimodal protocols, especially with benzodiazepines for induction quality and with alpha-2/opioid adjuncts for restraint or balanced anaesthesia.

commonUse: Common inhalational maintenance agent in dogs, cats, horses, rabbits, and exotics when rapid adjustment of anaesthetic depth is helpful.
Often preferred where smoother/faster inhalational changes and recovery are desirable.
avoidUse: Use carefully in hypotensive or unstable patients because it still causes dose-dependent cardiovascular and respiratory depression.
Inhalation induction is still not routine best practice in common domestic species unless a case-specific reason exists.
alternatives: Choose isoflurane when cost, availability, or a straightforward standard maintenance protocol matters more than faster depth changes.
Use injectable induction/maintenance plans or stronger multimodal sparing strategies if you want to reduce volatile requirement.
entities.whyChoose: Compared with isoflurane, sevoflurane is usually chosen when faster adjustment of depth and a lower blood-gas solubility profile are clinically useful.

entities.name: Thiopental
contraindications: use caution in cardiovascular compromise

entities.notes: Ultra-short-acting barbiturate induction agent.
sourceMentions: Equine Sedation and Anaesthesia in the Field.pptx
commonUse: Ultra-short-acting injectable induction agent most associated with older protocols and some equine or field anaesthesia teaching.
Useful when rapid IV induction is needed and the protocol specifically calls for a barbiturate induction approach.
avoidUse: Use carefully in hypovolaemic, hypotensive, or cardiovascularly compromised patients because dose-dependent cardiorespiratory depression can be clinically important.
Avoid perivascular administration because tissue irritation and sloughing risk are significant.
alternatives: Choose propofol or alfaxalone for more contemporary small-animal IV induction in many routine dog and cat cases.
Use ketamine-based or balanced induction plans when you want a different cardiovascular profile or more multimodal support.
entities.whyChoose: Compared with propofol or alfaxalone, thiopental is more of an older/field-context induction drug rather than the routine first-line small-animal default.

entities.name: Tiletamine-zolazepam
contraindications: recoveries may be prolonged or rough in some patients
dose carefully in hepatic compromise and species where prolonged recovery is undesirable
entities.notes: Fixed dissociative-benzodiazepine combination used in selected small-animal and exotic contexts.
entities.mechanism: Dissociative NMDA antagonism combined with benzodiazepine GABA-A facilitation
entities.mechanismGroup: Dissociative NMDA antagonism combined with benzodiazepine GABA-A facilitation
commonUse: Useful for chemical restraint or short injectable anaesthesia in selected species, especially where a fixed dissociative-benzodiazepine combination is taught.
Can be relevant in exotic or difficult-handling contexts depending on the case and formulation.
avoidUse: Use carefully where prolonged or dysphoric recovery would be a major problem.
Use carefully in significant hepatic compromise or where you need especially fine control over depth and recovery quality.
alternatives: Choose propofol or alfaxalone for smoother titratable IV induction in routine dogs and cats.
Choose ketamine combined with a separately chosen benzodiazepine or alpha-2 agent when you want more flexibility in the protocol.
entities.whyChoose: Compared with propofol or alfaxalone, tiletamine-zolazepam is more useful when practical chemical restraint and a fixed combination are priorities.

commonUse: Commonly discussed for sedation, restraint, and standing procedures in horses and ruminants.
Relevant in field and farm-animal protocols where an alpha-2 agonist sedative is needed.
avoidUse: Use carefully in animals with major cardiovascular compromise, marked respiratory compromise, or where species-specific alpha-2 sensitivity is a concern.
Dose carefully in ruminants because they can be very sensitive to alpha-2 effects.
alternatives: Choose detomidine or romifidine in horses when a different equine alpha-2 profile is preferred.
Choose medetomidine or dexmedetomidine in dogs and cats when you want the more familiar small-animal alpha-2 options.
entities.whyChoose: Compared with medetomidine or dexmedetomidine, xylazine is most associated with large-animal and mixed-species sedation teaching rather than routine small-animal premedication.
